scaling_analysis_stage_d.crossover_estimate: ε ≈ 0.001 (1000 classical samples vs ~785 quantum queries) — but requires ~100k+ physical qubits
scaling_analysis_stage_d.honest_assessment: Quadratic speedup is provable but practical advantage requires efficient amplitude encoding. Current O(2^n) state preparation circuit eliminates speedup for structured distributions.

scaling_summary.crossover_estimate: ε ≈ 0.001 (1000 classical samples vs ~785 quantum queries) — but requires ~100k+ physical qubits
scaling_summary.honest_assessment: Quadratic speedup is provable but practical advantage requires efficient amplitude encoding. Current O(2^n) state preparation circuit eliminates speedup for structured distributions.

stage_d_evidence_summary.recommendation: Stage D evidence package is complete. All claims are bounded by explicit residual risks and honest assessments. No demonstration of practical quantum advantage is claimed.

advantage_claim_contract.problem_class: Tail risk estimation: P(Loss > threshold) for continuous loss distributions
advantage_claim_contract.baseline_algorithm: Classical Monte Carlo with 10,000 samples (±0.39% at 95% CI)
advantage_claim_contract.fairness_rationale: MC is the standard industry method for tail risk; variance-reduced MC included for comparison
advantage_claim_contract.quantum_resource_scaling: QAE achieves O(1/ε) vs classical MC O(1/ε²) — quadratic speedup in precision
advantage_claim_contract.data_loading_assumptions: Amplitude encoding via multiplex rotations; exponential circuit depth O(2^n) for n-qubit encoding
advantage_claim_contract.noise_model_assumptions: Noiseless simulator; real hardware would require error correction for 40+ logical qubits
advantage_claim_contract.confidence_method: 20-run calibration ensemble with 95% Clopper-Pearson confidence intervals
residual_risks: Amplitude encoding circuit depth scales exponentially — practical advantage requires efficient state preparation
293k physical qubits required — beyond current NISQ devices
No noise model applied; fault-tolerant execution assumed
Classical importance sampling may close the gap for structured distributions

scaling_analysis_stage_d.classical_complexity: Brute-force O(2^n); GW SDP O(n³) with 0.878-approx
scaling_analysis_stage_d.quantum_complexity: QAOA depth O(n²) per layer, approximation ratio depends on depth p
scaling_analysis_stage_d.theoretical_speedup: None proven for constant-depth QAOA on MaxCut
scaling_analysis_stage_d.crossover_estimate: Uncertain — no proven quantum advantage for MaxCut QAOA at any depth
scaling_analysis_stage_d.honest_assessment: QAOA is a heuristic. GW achieves 0.878-approximation in polynomial time. No constant-depth QAOA is known to surpass this. Advantage would require super-polynomial circuit depth, which eliminates the speed benefit.

scaling_summary.quantum_complexity: QAOA depth O(n²) per layer, approximation ratio depends on depth p
scaling_summary.theoretical_speedup: None proven for constant-depth QAOA on MaxCut
scaling_summary.crossover_estimate: Uncertain — no proven quantum advantage for MaxCut QAOA at any depth
scaling_summary.honest_assessment: QAOA is a heuristic. GW achieves 0.878-approximation in polynomial time. No constant-depth QAOA is known to surpass this. Advantage would require super-polynomial circuit depth, which eliminates the speed benefit.

stage_d_evidence_summary.recommendation: Stage D evidence package is complete. All claims are bounded by explicit residual risks and honest assessments. No demonstration of practical quantum advantage is claimed.

advantage_claim_contract.problem_class: Maximum cut in weighted graphs (NP-hard combinatorial optimization)
advantage_claim_contract.instance_regime: Triangle graph (3 vertices, 3 edges), depth-1 QAOA
advantage_claim_contract.baseline_algorithm: Brute-force enumeration (optimal for n≤20); Goemans-Williamson SDP (0.878-approx for large n)
advantage_claim_contract.fairness_rationale: At n=3, brute-force trivially finds optimal; GW guarantee only relevant at n>30
advantage_claim_contract.quantum_resource_scaling: QAOA expected ratio approaches optimal as depth p→∞; constant-depth advantage unproven for MaxCut
advantage_claim_contract.data_loading_assumptions: Graph encoded directly as ZZ interactions; no data loading overhead
advantage_claim_contract.noise_model_assumptions: Noiseless simulator; real QAOA at depth>1 highly sensitive to gate noise
advantage_claim_contract.confidence_method: 20-run calibration ensemble; deterministic optimal found (ratio=1.0) on all runs
residual_risks: No proven quantum advantage for MaxCut QAOA at any constant depth
Classical GW algorithm achieves 0.878-approximation in polynomial time
Instance is trivially small (n=3) — advantage claims meaningless at this scale
131k physical qubits for a 3-vertex graph; scaling to practical graphs unknown

scaling_analysis_stage_d.quantum_complexity: O(√N) queries (provably optimal — BBBV lower bound)
scaling_analysis_stage_d.crossover_estimate: N ≈ 10^6 with structured oracle; N ≈ 10^12 with naive oracle (due to compilation overhead)
scaling_analysis_stage_d.honest_assessment: Grover speedup is provably optimal but quadratic. The oracle compilation cost is the critical variable: a naive oracle implementing the function as a circuit costs O(N) gates, completely eliminating the speedup. Only structured oracles with O(poly(n)) gate cost preserve the advantage.

scaling_summary.quantum_complexity: O(√N) queries (provably optimal — BBBV lower bound)
scaling_summary.crossover_estimate: N ≈ 10^6 with structured oracle; N ≈ 10^12 with naive oracle (due to compilation overhead)
scaling_summary.honest_assessment: Grover speedup is provably optimal but quadratic. The oracle compilation cost is the critical variable: a naive oracle implementing the function as a circuit costs O(N) gates, completely eliminating the speedup. Only structured oracles with O(poly(n)) gate cost preserve the advantage.

stage_d_evidence_summary.recommendation: Stage D evidence package is complete. All claims are bounded by explicit residual risks and honest assessments. No demonstration of practical quantum advantage is claimed.

advantage_claim_contract.instance_regime: N=16 (4 qubits), single target (M=1), 3 Grover iterations
advantage_claim_contract.baseline_algorithm: Classical linear search O(N); expected N/2 queries on average
advantage_claim_contract.fairness_rationale: Linear search is optimal for unstructured search — no classical heuristic can beat O(N)
advantage_claim_contract.quantum_resource_scaling: Grover achieves O(√N) queries — provably optimal quadratic speedup (BBBV lower bound)
advantage_claim_contract.data_loading_assumptions: Oracle assumed to be a black-box function; implementation cost O(n) for n-qubit register
advantage_claim_contract.noise_model_assumptions: Noiseless simulator; Grover amplitude amplification degrades significantly with gate errors
advantage_claim_contract.confidence_method: 20-run calibration ensemble; target found deterministically on all runs (search space N=16)
residual_risks: Quadratic speedup offset by large constant factors in fault-tolerant implementation
Oracle compilation cost not included — real oracles may require O(N) gates, eliminating speedup
119k physical qubits for 4-qubit search; scaling to useful N requires millions of qubits
Grover is provably optimal but constant-factor overhead may delay practical advantage to N>10^6

fairness_review_stage_d.baseline_optimality: Linear search is provably optimal for unstructured search (information-theoretic lower bound). No classical algorithm can do better than O(N) for a truly unstructured search.
fairness_review_stage_d.fairness_assessment: FAIR — linear search is the strongest possible classical comparator for unstructured search. This is the rare case where the classical baseline cannot be improved by heuristics.

instance_analysis.note: Meaningful speedup but classical is still fast

oracle_cost_sensitivity.description: The fairness of the comparison critically depends on oracle implementation cost
oracle_cost_sensitivity.structured_oracle: O(n) = O(log N) gates → preserves quadratic speedup
oracle_cost_sensitivity.practical_implication: Advantage exists only for problems with efficiently implementable oracles (e.g., cryptographic hash verification, SAT checking)

residual_unfairness_risks: Classical parallel search on k processors achieves O(N/k) — linear parallelism may close the gap
Classical cache-friendly memory access patterns provide constant-factor advantages not captured in query complexity
Grover requires coherent oracle evaluation — decoherence may require error correction overhead not in the query count
